question: Explain the Double Diamond model of design process and its four phases.
expectedPoints: First diamond: Discover (research, user insights) and Define (synthesize findings)
Second diamond: Develop (ideate, prototype solutions) and Deliver (finalize, launch)
Alternates between divergent (exploration) and convergent (decision-making) thinking
Provides structured framework for design thinking

question: What is a design system? List its key components.
expectedPoints: Definition: Collection of reusable components and guidelines
Key components: Design tokens, component library, style guide, pattern library
Purpose: Ensures consistency across products
Benefits: Faster development, reduced errors, unified brand experience

question: Describe the differences between low-fidelity and high-fidelity prototypes.
expectedPoints: Low-fidelity: Simple wireframes, focus on layout and structure, often paper-based
High-fidelity: Detailed mockups with colors, typography, interactions
Low-fidelity used early for concept validation
High-fidelity used later for user testing and developer handoff

question: How does Agile methodology integrate design into the development process?
expectedPoints: Design happens continuously throughout all sprints
Designers work alongside developers in each iteration
User feedback is incorporated rapidly in subsequent sprints
Eliminates large upfront design phase (vs Waterfall)
Promotes collaboration and reduces handoff problems

question: What are design tokens and why are they important for developer-designer collaboration?
expectedPoints: Design tokens are visual design variables (colors, spacing, fonts)
Stored as code that can be shared between design and development tools
Ensures consistency: changes in token update all instances automatically
Bridges gap between design specifications and implementation
Enables theming and responsive design easily

question: Explain what is meant by design debt and how it can be managed.
expectedPoints: Design debt: accumulated cost of design shortcuts or inconsistencies
Similar to technical debt but for design quality
Occurs when quick fixes create long-term maintainability issues
Management: Document design debt, prioritize refactoring, include design in technical reviews
Prevention: Use design systems, establish guidelines early

question: Discuss the relationship between UI/UX design and software engineering. How can organizations improve collaboration between designers and developers?
expectedPoints: Design and engineering are deeply interconnected - design decisions impact implementation
Designers must consider technical feasibility; engineers must understand user needs
Challenges: communication gaps, different vocabularies, handoff problems
Solutions: Cross-functional teams, design systems, Agile integration
Tools: Shared design and code repositories, design handoff tools
Process: Regular sync meetings, design reviews, pair sessions
Outcome: Better products, faster delivery, reduced rework

question: Explain the design handoff process. What information should be included in design specifications to ensure accurate implementation?
expectedPoints: Design handoff: process of transferring design to development team
Key elements: Wireframes/prototypes, visual mockups, style guides
Must include: Color codes, typography specs, spacing measurements
Interaction details: hover states, animations, transitions
Component states: default, active, disabled, error states
Responsive behavior for different screen sizes
Assets: Icons, images in appropriate formats
Tools: Figma, Zeplin, InVision for collaboration
Best practice: Ongoing communication, not one-time transfer

question: Describe the various UX testing methods and when each should be used in the development lifecycle.
expectedPoints: Usability testing: Real users performing tasks, conducted throughout
A/B testing: Comparing two versions, used for optimization
Card sorting: Information architecture, used early
Tree testing: Navigation structure validation
Eye tracking: Understanding visual attention, specialized
Survey/questionnaires: Gathering quantitative feedback
Prototype testing: At mockup stage before development
Beta testing: With real users after basic functionality
Frequency: Test early and often, especially after major changes

question: How does User-Centered Design (UCD) methodology influence software engineering decisions?
expectedPoints: UCD places user needs at the center of all decisions
Engineering must implement accessible, intuitive interfaces
Requirements gathering includes user research, not just stakeholder input
Iterative development allows for user feedback incorporation
Usability becomes a quality metric alongside performance
Trade-offs between features and usability must be evaluated
Cross-functional teams ensure user perspective throughout
Results in more successful, adopted products
